# Large section fixture

## Performance Tuning

Performance tuning a MERN web application typically begins with measurement, not optimization. Before adding caching layers or rewriting database queries, you should establish a baseline using a real workload. Tools like autocannon, wrk, and k6 can drive synthetic traffic at the backend, while Lighthouse and WebPageTest give you frontend metrics. Capture the median and the 95th percentile of response time, the throughput in requests per second, and the error rate at peak load. Save the baseline to a markdown file in the repo so future changes can be compared against a known reference. Without this baseline you cannot tell whether a code change improved or regressed performance, and you will end up arguing about feelings instead of numbers in pull request reviews. The single highest-leverage optimization in most MongoDB-backed applications is adding the right indexes. The default index on the id field is fine for primary-key lookups, but any query that filters on user, product slug, order status, or createdAt will scan the entire collection if no secondary index exists. Use the explain executionStats output to confirm that a query uses an index and to read the number of documents examined; the goal is for totalDocsExamined to approach nReturned. Compound indexes follow the equality-sort-range rule: the equality fields come first, then the sort field, then the range field. An index on user, status, createdAt covers a query like list this user pending orders sorted by date with a single scan, while the same fields in a different order can force a full collection scan even though every individual field is indexed. Slow queries that cannot be fixed with an index are usually caused by unbounded result sets, and a list endpoint that returns every document in a collection will degrade as the data grows. Beyond indexing, query rewriting often surfaces wins: replacing a chain of populate calls with a single aggregation pipeline reduces round trips, and projecting only the needed fields keeps the working set small enough to fit in cache. Profile every endpoint that touches the database under realistic load, not just the obvious ones.

The fix to unbounded queries is pagination: every list endpoint must accept a page size and a page number, with hard upper bounds enforced server-side. Cursor-based pagination using the last seen id is more robust than skip-and-limit for large collections, because skip becomes linear in the offset and the database has to walk past every skipped document. The frontend should default to a page size of twenty or fifty and let the user request more explicitly. Returning a total count field is convenient for the UI but expensive on large collections; consider returning an approximate count or omitting it entirely on hot paths. Caching at the application layer pays off when the same data is read many times more often than it changes. Redis is the typical choice; the cache key is derived from the route plus a hash of the query parameters, and the value is the serialized JSON response. The time-to-live should match the freshness requirement of the endpoint: product catalogue data can be cached for ten minutes, the current user cart cannot be cached at all. Cache invalidation is the hard part: write operations must explicitly delete the keys they affect, or the staleness will leak into the UI and confuse users. A second pattern, the stale-while-revalidate cache, returns the old value immediately while triggering a background refresh; this hides backend latency at the cost of a short window of stale data, which is acceptable for most catalogue and listing endpoints. Frontend performance has its own measurement and tuning loop. The Largest Contentful Paint, First Input Delay, and Cumulative Layout Shift are the three Core Web Vitals that Google reports and that users actually notice. Reducing bundle size with code splitting, lazy loading routes, and tree shaking unused dependencies often gives the largest wins for First Contentful Paint. Audit every npm dependency for size and necessity: a moment-with-locales import can balloon the bundle by hundreds of kilobytes when a date-fns subset would do. Run a bundle analyzer on every release candidate and treat any unexplained growth as a regression worth investigating before merging.

Server-side rendering or static generation removes the blank-screen latency that single-page apps inherit by default. Image optimization through responsive srcset, modern formats like AVIF, and explicit width and height attributes prevents layout shift and saves bandwidth on slow networks. Defer non-critical JavaScript with the defer or async attribute so the browser can parse and render the initial HTML before executing third-party scripts that often dominate the timeline. Network-level tuning is sometimes overlooked but pays off for global users. Putting the static assets behind a CDN cuts the time-to-first-byte from hundreds of milliseconds to tens. HTTP/2 multiplexing replaces the head-of-line blocking that plagued HTTP/1.1, and HTTP/3 over QUIC adds resilience to flaky mobile connections. Brotli compression beats gzip by fifteen to twenty percent on typical JSON and HTML payloads. Keep-alive on the backend connection pool eliminates the handshake cost when the same client makes many requests in quick succession. Combine these and the perceived latency drops dramatically without changing a single line of business logic. Profiling in production tells you what the synthetic benchmarks miss. Node.js exposes a built-in inspector that you can attach to a running process, capture a CPU profile for thirty seconds, and analyze the flamegraph for hot functions. APM tools like Datadog, New Relic, or open-source alternatives such as OpenTelemetry plus Jaeger trace requests across services and identify the database call or external API that dominates the tail latency. Memory profiling catches leaks early: a heap snapshot taken every hour and diffed against the previous one reveals retained objects that should have been freed. Database connection pools sit at the boundary between the application tier and the data tier and quietly limit throughput when misconfigured. The pool size must match the database server max connections divided by the number of dynos. Setting it too low queues requests at the dyno; setting it too high exhausts the server and causes connection-refused errors that look like network problems. Tune the pool with measurement, not guesswork.
